   119→Use 8px spacing grid. Use modular typography scale (1.25 or 1.333 ratio).
   120→
   121→## Web-Specific Anti-Patterns
   122→
   123→| Anti-Pattern | Alternative |
   124→|-------------|-------------|
   125→| Autoplay video with sound | Mute by default, user-initiated play |
   126→| Full-page interstitials on mobile | Inline or bottom-sheet prompts |
   127→| Captchas for every action | Risk-based authentication, invisible captchas |
   128→| Horizontal scrolling for content | Responsive layout fitting viewport |
   129→| Form validation only on submit | Inline validation with reward-early-punish-late |
   130→| Mystery meat navigation (icons only) | Label all nav items; icons supplement, not replace |
   131→| Stacking modals | Inline expansion or navigate to new page |
   132→
   133→## Acceptance Criteria Template (Web)
   134→
   135→```gherkin
   136→Feature: [Feature Name]
   137→  # Platform: web
   138→  # Key heuristics: [applicable Nielsen heuristics]
   139→  # Accessibility: WCAG 2.2 AA
   140→
   141→  Scenario: Happy path
   142→    Given [context]
   143→    When [user action]
   144→    Then [expected visible result]
   145→    And system provides feedback within 100ms
   146→    And the action can be undone via [mechanism]
   147→
   148→  Scenario: Error state
   149→    Given [context leading to error]
   150→    When [user action that triggers error]
   151→    Then an error message explains what happened
   152→    And the error message suggests what to do next
   153→    And the error is shown inline next to the relevant field
   154→
   155→  Scenario: Empty state
   156→    Given no [resources] exist yet
   157→    When the user navigates to the [resource] view
   158→    Then a helpful message explains what [resources] are
   159→    And a clear call to action creates the first one
   160→
   161→  Scenario: Keyboard accessibility
   162→    Given the user navigates with keyboard only
   163→    When they tab through the interface
   164→    Then all interactive elements are reachable
   165→    And focus indicators are visible
   166→```
   167→

1→---
     2→name: nw-ux-emotional-design
     3→description: Emotional design and delight patterns for product owners. Load when designing onboarding flows, empty states, first-run experiences, or evaluating the emotional quality of an interface.
     4→user-invocable: false
     5→disable-model-invocation: true
     6→---
     7→
     8→# Emotional Design and Delight
     9→
    10→Patterns for creating interfaces that feel good to use. Use during discovery to map emotional arcs and during requirements to specify delight moments.
    11→
    12→## Walter's Hierarchy of User Needs
    13→
    14→Four layers satisfied in order (bottom to top):
    15→
    16→```
    17→        /\
    18→       /  \
    19→      / PL \     4. PLEASURABLE - Delight, surprise, emotional connection
    20→     /------\
    21→    / USABLE \    3. USABLE - Easy to learn, intuitive, efficient
    22→   /----------\
    23→  / RELIABLE   \   2. RELIABLE - Consistent, dependable, no crashes
    24→ /--------------\
    25→/ FUNCTIONAL     \  1. FUNCTIONAL - It works, serves its purpose
    26→------------------
    27→```
    28→
    29→**Key insight**: Delight only works if product is usable. Beautiful animations on buggy, confusing interface make things worse. Invest in foundation before polish.
    30→
    31→### Applying the Hierarchy in Requirements
    32→- Phase 1 stories: functionality works correctly
    33→- Phase 2 stories: reliability (error handling, edge cases, recovery)
    34→- Phase 3 stories: usability (simplify flows, reduce steps)
    35→- Phase 4 stories: delight (only after above are solid)
    36→
    37→## Surface Delight vs Deep Delight
    38→
    39→### Surface Delight (momentary, contextual)
    40→Playful animations | witty microcopy | surprising easter eggs | visually pleasing illustrations
    41→
    42→### Deep Delight (sustained, holistic)
    43→Interface anticipates user needs | complex tasks feel effortless | users achieve flow state | tool becomes extension of thinking
    44→
    45→**Prioritization rule**: Deep delight generates loyalty and return usage. Surface delight creates momentary reactions but cannot compensate for usability failures. Invest in deep delight first.
    46→
    47→### Requirements Implications
    48→- "System suggests most likely next action" (deep) is higher priority than "Save button has satisfying animation" (surface)
    49→- Stories reducing steps, anticipating needs, removing friction are delight stories even if they don't feel "fun"
    50→
    51→## Empty States
    52→
    53→Empty states (no data, first use, zero results) are opportunities, not dead ends.
    54→
    55→### Good Empty State Design
    56→- Explain what will appear when there is content
    57→- Clear call to action to create first item
    58→- Illustration or visual interest making state feel intentional
    59→- Guidance or templates for first-time users
    60→
    61→### Anti-Pattern
    62→Blank page with no guidance, or "No results found" with no suggested next step.
    63→
    64→### Empty State Checklist for Requirements
    65→- [ ] First-time empty state has onboarding guidance
    66→- [ ] Search empty state suggests alternative queries or filters
    67→- [ ] Error empty state explains what happened and how to recover
    68→- [ ] Each empty state has primary call to action
    69→
    70→## Onboarding and First-Run Experience
    71→
    72→### Progressive Onboarding (preferred)
    73→- Let users start real work immediately
    74→- Introduce features in context, when relevant
    75→- Tooltips and inline hints dismissing after first use
    76→- "Skip" option for experienced users
    77→- Celebrate first successful action
    78→
    79→### Anti-Pattern
    80→Mandatory 8-step walkthrough blocking users from doing anything before completion.
    81→
    82→### Onboarding Patterns by Platform
    83→
    84→**Web**: Inline hints | contextual tooltips | sample data to explore | "getting started" checklist widget
    85→
    86→**Desktop**: First-run wizard for essential setup only (account, preferences), then contextual hints during use
    87→
    88→**CLI**: First command outputs welcome message with 2-3 example commands. `--help` is comprehensive. Config file created with sensible defaults and comments.
    89→
    90→## Tone of Voice in UI Copy
    91→
    92→### Principles
    93→Clear first, clever second | active voice, present tense | address user as "you" | instructions in 1-2 sentences | consistent voice builds trust
    94→
    95→### Matching Tone to Context
    96→
    97→| Context | Tone | Example |
    98→|---------|------|---------|
    99→| Error message | Empathetic, helpful | "We could not save your changes. Check your connection and try again." |
   100→| Success message | Encouraging, brief | "Project created. You are ready to start." |
   101→| Empty state | Inviting, guiding | "No projects yet. Create your first one to get started." |
   102→| Destructive action | Clear, serious | "This will permanently delete 3 files. This cannot be undone." |
   103→| Loading/waiting | Reassuring | "Setting things up. This usually takes about 30 seconds." |
   104→| Neutral action | Straightforward | "Select a template." |
   105→
   106→### When Personality Helps vs Annoys
   107→
   108→**Helps when**: user not stressed (onboarding, success, empty states) | low-stakes moment | brand voice well-established
   109→
   110→**Annoys when**: user frustrated (errors, failures) | user in a hurry (critical workflows) | humor forced or inconsistent | cleverness obscures message
   111→
   112→## Microinteractions That Create Delight
   113→
   114→### High-Value Microinteractions
   115→Pull-to-refresh with satisfying animation | skeleton screens instead of blank loading | smooth state transitions (not abrupt swaps) | smart defaults reducing typing | autocomplete learning from usage | undo toast after destructive actions ("Deleted. Undo?")
   116→
   117→### Low-Value Microinteractions (skip these)
   118→Decorative loading animations adding no information | sound effects for routine actions | excessive bounce/wobble animations | easter eggs interfering with workflow
   119→
   120→### Requirements Pattern for Microinteractions
   121→Specify: trigger (what user does) | feedback (what user sees/feels) | purpose (why this matters for experience)
   122→
   123→Example: "When user drags card to new column, card smoothly animates to new position and column header count updates, confirming move was successful."
   124→
   125→## Emotional Arc Integration
   126→
   127→When mapping emotional arcs during journey discovery:
   128→
   129→| Journey Phase | Target Emotion | Design Lever |
   130→|--------------|----------------|--------------|
   131→| First encounter | Curious, welcomed | Clear value proposition, inviting empty state |
   132→| Setup/config | Confident, guided | Progressive onboarding, sensible defaults |
   133→| First success | Accomplished, delighted | Celebration moment, clear confirmation |
   134→| Regular use | Efficient, in flow | Shortcuts, anticipation, minimal friction |
   135→| Error/failure | Supported, not blamed | Empathetic copy, clear recovery path |
   136→| Completion | Satisfied, proud | Summary of accomplishment, next steps |
   137→
   138→Use this table when asking emotional arc questions during discovery. Map each journey step to target emotion and identify design lever that achieves it.
   139→

1→---
     2→name: nw-outcome-kpi-framework
     3→description: Outcome KPI definition methodology - synthesizes Who Does What By How Much (Gothelf/Seiden), Running Lean (Maurya), and Measure What Matters (Doerr) into a practical framework for measurable outcome KPIs
     4→user-invocable: false
     5→disable-model-invocation: true
     6→---
     7→
     8→# Outcome KPI Framework
     9→
    10→"Doing stuff isn't the point. Achieving stuff is." -- Jeff Gothelf
    11→
    12→Defines measurable outcome KPIs for user stories and features. Loaded during Phase 4 (Requirements Crafting) to produce `outcome-kpis.md`. Synthesizes three frameworks: customer-centric OKRs, lean metrics, and OKR methodology.
    13→
    14→## The Outcome KPI Formula
    15→
    16→Primary template from Gothelf/Seiden. Every KPI answers five questions:
    17→
    18→| Component | Question | Example |
    19→|-----------|----------|---------|
    20→| **Who** | Which user segment? | Returning customers with 2+ orders |
    21→| **Does What** | What observable behavior changes? | Complete checkout without contacting support |
    22→| **By How Much** | What is the measurable target? | 40% reduction in support tickets |
    23→| **Measured By** | How do we collect the data? | Support ticket system + checkout analytics |
    24→| **Timeframe** | When do we measure? | 30 days post-release, then weekly |
    25→
    26→Formula: **[Who] [Does what] [By how much]**
    27→
    28→Apply as litmus test: if a KPI cannot answer all five components, it measures an output (feature delivery), not an outcome (behavior change).
    29→
    30→### Good vs Bad KPIs
    31→
    32→| Bad (Output) | Good (Outcome) |
    33→|-------------|----------------|
    34→| Launch mobile app v2 | Mobile users complete purchases 40% more often |
    35→| Build recommendation engine | Users purchase from recommendations, increasing from 10% to 25% |
    36→| Deploy onboarding redesign | New users complete onboarding within 24 hours 30% more often |
    37→| Ship CSV export | Analysts resolve data questions without engineering support 60% of the time |
    38→
    39→## Leading vs Lagging Indicators
    40→
    41→From Gothelf/Seiden: business results are lagging -- teams cannot directly influence them. Target leading indicators instead.
    42→
    43→| Type | Definition | Examples | Actionable? |
    44→|------|-----------|----------|-------------|
    45→| **Lagging** (Impact) | Business results already happened | Revenue, NPS, market share, churn rate | No -- too slow, too many variables |
    46→| **Leading** (Outcome) | Behavior changes predicting business results | Purchase completion rate, feature adoption, retention | Yes -- teams can run experiments |
    47→| **Leading** (Secondary) | Behaviors predicting primary leading indicators | Page visits, trial starts, onboarding steps completed | Yes -- most granular, fastest signal |
    48→
    49→### Outcome Mapping Chain
    50→
    51→Map every KPI through this chain to ensure traceability:
    52→
    53→```
    54→Business KPI (Lagging/Impact)
    55→    Example: "Increase quarterly revenue by 15%"
    56→        |
    57→        v
    58→    Customer Behavior (Leading/Outcome)
    59→        +-- Users complete purchases from recommendations (+25%)
    60→        +-- Users return within 7 days (+20%)
    61→        |
    62→        v
    63→    Secondary Behavior (Leading/Secondary)
    64→        +-- Users browse recommendation pages (+30%)
    65→        +-- Users enable push notifications (+15%)
    66→```
    67→
    68→Each layer decomposes into more granular behavioral metrics. Teams target the highest-leverage behavior.
    69→
    70→## Actionable vs Vanity Metrics
    71→
    72→From Maurya (Running Lean): actionable metrics "tie specific and repeatable actions to observed results."
    73→
    74→| Dimension | Vanity | Actionable |
    75→|-----------|--------|------------|
    76→| Measures | Business size (totals) | Individual behavior (rates) |
    77→| Data type | Gross aggregates | Ratios and unit economics |
    78→| Cause/effect | No insight into why | Directly signal product-market fit |
    79→| Examples | Total users, page views, downloads | Activation rate, retention cohort, churn rate |
    80→| Decision value | Cannot inform action | Drives specific experiments |
    81→
    82→### The OMTM (One Metric That Matters)
    83→
    84→Pick ONE metric per product stage. Optimizing one metric reveals the next.
    85→
    86→| Stage | Focus | Example OMTM |
    87→|-------|-------|--------------|
    88→| Empathy | Problem validation | Interview pain intensity (qualitative) |
    89→| Stickiness | Retention | Churn rate, DAU/MAU ratio |
    90→| Virality | Organic growth | Viral coefficient, referral rate |
    91→| Revenue | Monetization | Customer Lifetime Value, MRR |
    92→| Scale | Growth efficiency | CAC/LTV ratio, payback period |
    93→
    94→**Good metric characteristics**: rate or ratio (not absolute number) | comparable across time | simple enough to remember | predictive | behavior-changing.
    95→
    96→### Customer Factory (AARRR) Constraint Mapping
    97→
    98→From Maurya: model the business as a production line. Identify the bottleneck, then focus KPIs there.
    99→
   100→| Stage | Key Question | Example Metric |
   101→|-------|-------------|----------------|
   102→| **Acquisition** | Are we reaching the right people? | Visitor-to-signup conversion rate |
   103→| **Activation** | Do users get the "aha moment"? | % completing core action in first session |
   104→| **Retention** | Do users come back? | Week-1 return rate, DAU/MAU |
   105→| **Revenue** | Do users pay? | Trial-to-paid conversion rate |
   106→| **Referral** | Do users tell others? | Referral rate, viral coefficient |
   107→
   108→Activation is causal -- it drives retention, revenue, and referral. Prioritize activation KPIs when uncertain.
   109→
   110→## OKR Integration
   111→
   112→From Doerr (Measure What Matters): connect KPIs to strategic objectives.
   113→
   114→### Writing Key Results
   115→
   116→Every Key Result uses the outcome formula. Quality criteria:
   117→
   118→1. **Measurable**: "It's not a Key Result unless it has a number" (Marissa Mayer)
   119→2. **Outcome-focused**: "Increase email subscribers by 20%" not "Launch newsletter"
   120→3. **Time-bound**: deadline (typically end of quarter)
   121→4. **Verifiable**: no ambiguity about whether met
   122→5. **Aggressive yet realistic**: stretch without demoralizing
   123→
   124→### Committed vs Aspirational
   125→
   126→| Type | Expected Score | Resource Allocation | Failure Response |
   127→|------|---------------|--------------------|-----------------|
   128→| **Committed** | 1.0 (must deliver) | Consume most available resources | Requires explanation, replanning |
   129→| **Aspirational** | 0.7 (stretch goal) | Overcommit slightly beyond capacity | Expected -- carry forward |
   130→
   131→Sweet spot: blended aggregate of 0.6-0.7. Consistently hitting 1.0 = not ambitious enough.
   132→
   133→### OKR Anti-Patterns
   134→
   135→| Anti-Pattern | Signal | Fix |
   136→|-------------|--------|-----|
   137→| Output-based KRs | "Launch X", "Build Y", "Ship Z" | Rewrite as behavior: "Users [do what] [by how much]" |
   138→| Too many KRs | >5 KRs per Objective | Cut to 2-4 per Objective, max 3-5 Objectives |
   139→| Vague KRs | No numeric target | Add baseline + target + deadline |
   140→| Sandbagging | Consistently scoring 1.0 | Increase ambition level |
   141→| Backlog retrofitting | OKRs match existing backlog 1:1 | OKRs filter backlog, not justify it |
   142→
   143→### Mapping: Objective to User Stories
   144→
   145→```
   146→Objective (qualitative, inspirational, timeboxed)
   147→    |
   148→    Key Results (2-4 per Objective, [Who][Does what][By how much])
   149→    |
   150→    Epics (weeks of work, aligned to Key Results)
   151→    |
   152→    User Stories (days of work, with measurable acceptance criteria)
   153→```
   154→
   155→Every story traces back to a Key Result. Orphan stories (no KR link) are potential waste.
   156→
   157→## KPI Template
   158→
   159→Use this exact structure in `outcome-kpis.md`:
   160→
   161→```markdown
   162→## Feature: {feature-name}
   163→
   164→### Objective
   165→{What success looks like in one sentence -- qualitative, inspirational, timeboxed}
   166→
   167→### Outcome KPIs
   168→
   169→| # | Who | Does What | By How Much | Baseline | Measured By | Type |
   170→|---|-----|-----------|-------------|----------|-------------|------|
   171→| 1 | {segment} | {behavior} | {target} | {current} | {method} | Leading/Lagging |
   172→
   173→### Metric Hierarchy
   174→- **North Star**: {the ONE metric that matters most for this feature}
   175→- **Leading Indicators**: {behaviors that predict the north star}
   176→- **Guardrail Metrics**: {metrics that must NOT degrade}
   177→
   178→### Measurement Plan
   179→| KPI | Data Source | Collection Method | Frequency | Owner |
   180→|-----|------------|-------------------|-----------|-------|
   181→
   182→### Hypothesis
   183→We believe that {proposed solution} for {user segment} will achieve {key result}.
   184→We will know this is true when {who} {does what} {by how much}.
   185→```
   186→
   187→## KPI Granularity
   188→
   189→- **Per Epic**: Define 2-3 north-star KPIs that all contributing stories aim to move
   190→- **Per Story**: Add story-level success criteria tied to the epic-level KPIs
   191→- **Guardrails**: Define at epic level, apply consistently across all stories
   192→- **Rule of thumb**: If the feature has 1-3 stories, one KPI table suffices. If 4+, group by epic.
   193→
   194→## Smell Tests
   195→
   196→Before finalizing KPIs, verify each one passes:
   197→
   198→| Check | Question | If No |
   199→|-------|----------|-------|
   200→| Measurable today? | Can you measure it with current instrumentation? | Add instrumentation to requirements |
   201→| Rate not total? | Is it a ratio/rate, not a gross count? | Convert to rate (vanity -> actionable) |
   202→| Outcome not output? | Does it describe user behavior, not feature delivery? | Rewrite as "[Who] [Does what] [By how much]" |
   203→| Has baseline? | Do you know the current value? | Establish baseline before setting target |
   204→| Team can influence? | Can the team directly affect this metric? | Decompose into more granular leading indicator |
   205→| Has guardrails? | Are there metrics that must not degrade? | Add guardrail metrics (e.g., error rate, load time) |
   206→
   207→## Handoff to DEVOPS
   208→
   209→The platform-architect needs these from `outcome-kpis.md` to plan instrumentation:
   210→
   211→1. **Data collection requirements**: what events/behaviors to instrument, what data points to capture
   212→2. **Dashboard/monitoring needs**: which metrics need real-time dashboards vs. weekly reports
   213→3. **Alerting thresholds**: guardrail metric boundaries that trigger alerts when breached
   214→4. **Baseline measurement**: any metrics needing baseline collection before feature release
   215→
   216→## References
   217→
   218→For deeper reading on source frameworks:
   219→- Running Lean (Maurya): `docs/research/running-lean-research.md`
   220→- Measure What Matters (Doerr): `docs/research/measure-what-matters-research.md`
   221→- Who Does What By How Much (Gothelf/Seiden): `docs/research/who-does-what-research.md`
   222→

1→---
     2→name: nw-jtbd-bdd-integration
     3→description: Translating JTBD analysis to BDD scenarios - job story to Given-When-Then patterns, forces-based test discovery, job-map-based test discovery, and property-shaped criteria
     4→user-invocable: false
     5→disable-model-invocation: true
     6→---
     7→
     8→# JTBD to BDD Integration
     9→
    10→Use when translating JTBD discoveries (job stories, forces, job map) into testable BDD scenarios. Job stories map naturally to Given-When-Then because both emphasize context (situation/given), action (motivation/when), and outcome (result/then).
    11→
    12→## Job Story to Given-When-Then Translation
    13→
    14→### The Pattern
    15→
    16→```
    17→Job Story:
    18→  When [situation],
    19→  I want to [motivation],
    20→  so I can [outcome].
    21→
    22→BDD Scenario:
    23→  Given [the situation from the job story]
    24→  When [user takes action aligned with the motivation]
    25→  Then [the outcome is achieved and measurable]
    26→```
    27→
    28→### Worked Example
    29→
    30→**Job Story**: "When I receive an alert that a production service is degraded, I want to see the most likely root cause with supporting evidence, so I can begin remediation within minutes instead of hours."
    31→
    32→```gherkin
    33→Scenario: Root cause surfaced from degradation alert
    34→  Given the monitoring system has detected degraded response times on the payment service
    35→  And the degradation started 5 minutes ago
    36→  When the operator opens the incident dashboard
    37→  Then the system displays the top 3 probable root causes ranked by likelihood
    38→  And each root cause includes supporting metrics and log evidence
    39→  And the most likely root cause is displayed within 10 seconds
    40→
    41→Scenario: Remediation guidance from identified root cause
    42→  Given the operator has identified the root cause as database connection pool exhaustion
    43→  When the operator selects the recommended remediation action
    44→  Then the system presents a runbook with step-by-step instructions
    45→  And the estimated time to remediate is displayed
    46→  And the operator can execute the first step directly from the dashboard
    47→```
    48→
    49→### Translation Rules
    50→
    51→1. **Given** = situation from job story, made concrete with specific data
    52→2. **When** = user action fulfilling the motivation
    53→3. **Then** = measurable outcome, including quality attributes (time, confidence, completeness)
    54→
    55→### Multi-Scenario Expansion
    56→
    57→One job story typically produces 3-5 BDD scenarios:
    58→- **Happy path**: Outcome achieved as described
    59→- **Anxiety path**: User's fear about new solution addressed
    60→- **Habit path**: User transitioning from old workflow supported
    61→- **Edge cases**: Derived from job map steps (see below)
    62→
    63→## Forces-Based Test Discovery
    64→
    65→Use Four Forces to discover test scenarios that feature-driven thinking misses.
    66→
    67→### Force-to-Scenario Mapping
    68→
    69→| Force | Test Question | Scenario Pattern |
    70→|-------|--------------|-----------------|
    71→| **Push** | What frustration drove the user here? | `Given [user has experienced the frustration]...` |
    72→| **Pull** | What outcome is the user hoping for? | `Then [the promised improvement is measurable]` |
    73→| **Anxiety** | What could go wrong with the new solution? | `Given [user has specific fear] When [fear-triggering event] Then [safety net activates]` |
    74→| **Habit** | What existing workflow might resist adoption? | `Given [user accustomed to old way] When [new approach encountered] Then [familiar patterns preserved]` |
    75→
    76→### Worked Example: Automated Deployment Tool
    77→
    78→**Forces analysis**:
    79→- Push: Manual deployments cause errors and take hours
    80→- Pull: One-command deployment with automatic rollback
    81→- Anxiety: "What if the automated rollback breaks worse than the original deploy?"
    82→- Habit: Team SSH-es into servers and runs scripts manually
    83→
    84→**Derived scenarios**:
    85→
    86→```gherkin
    87→# Push scenario: validates the frustration is resolved
    88→Scenario: Deployment completes without manual intervention
    89→  Given the operator has configured deployment for the payment service
    90→  And the previous manual deployment took 3 hours with 2 errors
    91→  When the operator runs the automated deployment command
    92→  Then deployment completes within 15 minutes
    93→  And zero manual steps are required
    94→
    95→# Pull scenario: validates the promised outcome
    96→Scenario: Automatic rollback on health check failure
    97→  Given the deployment has completed and health checks are running
    98→  When the payment endpoint returns 500 errors for 30 seconds
    99→  Then the system automatically rolls back to the previous version
   100→  And the operator receives a notification with rollback details
   101→
   102→# Anxiety scenario: addresses the specific fear
   103→Scenario: Rollback preserves database state
   104→  Given the deployment included a database migration
   105→  And the health check has triggered an automatic rollback
   106→  When the rollback completes
   107→  Then the database schema matches the previous stable version
   108→  And no data is lost during the rollback process
   109→  And the operator can verify data integrity from the dashboard
   110→
   111→# Habit scenario: eases the transition
   112→Scenario: Manual override available during automated deployment
   113→  Given the operator is accustomed to SSH-based manual deployments
   114→  When the automated deployment is in progress
   115→  Then the operator can view real-time logs (familiar output format)
   116→  And the operator can pause or cancel the deployment at any stage
   117→  And manual SSH access remains available as a fallback
   118→```
   119→
   120→## Job-Map-Based Test Discovery
   121→
   122→Walk each of the 8 job map steps and generate at least one test scenario per step. Surfaces edge cases that happy-path thinking misses.
   123→
   124→| Job Map Step | Missing Scenario Pattern | Example |
   125→|-------------|------------------------|---------|
   126→| **Define** | User lacks information to start | `Given the user has not reviewed the change log When they attempt to deploy Then the system warns about unreviewed changes` |
   127→| **Locate** | Required inputs unavailable or stale | `Given the build artifact is 30 days old When the user selects it for deployment Then the system flags the artifact as potentially stale` |
   128→| **Prepare** | Setup fails or environment misconfigured | `Given the target environment has a port conflict When preparation runs Then the system reports the conflict with resolution steps` |
   129→| **Confirm** | Validation passes incorrectly (false positive) | `Given the pre-check passes but a dependency is actually down When deployment proceeds Then the system detects the issue at runtime and halts` |
   130→| **Execute** | Execution interrupted midway | `Given deployment is 50% complete When the network connection drops Then the system resumes from the last checkpoint after reconnection` |
   131→| **Monitor** | Monitoring reports misleading success | `Given all health checks pass but latency has doubled When the operator reviews the dashboard Then latency degradation is highlighted as a warning` |
   132→| **Modify** | Modification makes things worse | `Given the operator applies a hotfix during an incident When the hotfix introduces a new error Then the system detects the regression and suggests reverting` |
   133→| **Conclude** | Cleanup fails silently | `Given deployment succeeded and cleanup runs When temporary resources fail to delete Then the system logs the failure and alerts the operator` |
   134→
   135→### Application Process
   136→
   137→1. List the 8 job map steps for the feature
   138→2. For each step, ask: "What could go wrong here that we have not tested?"
   139→3. Write one scenario per step minimum
   140→4. Steps 1-4 (Define through Confirm) and 7-8 (Modify, Conclude) produce most overlooked scenarios
   141→
   142→## The @property Tag for Property-Shaped Criteria
   143→
   144→Some AC describe system properties rather than discrete scenarios -- ongoing qualities, not one-time events.
   145→
   146→### When to Use @property
   147→
   148→Use when criterion: describes quality holding continuously (not one scenario) | cannot be captured by finite Given-When-Then set | relates to performance, consistency, or invariants
   149→
   150→### Format
   151→
   152→```gherkin
   153→@property
   154→Scenario: Response time consistency under load
   155→  Given the system is handling 100 concurrent requests
   156→  Then all API responses complete within 200ms at the 95th percentile
   157→  And no response exceeds 1000ms
   158→
   159→@property
   160→Scenario: Data consistency across replicas
   161→  Given writes have been committed to the primary database
   162→  Then all read replicas reflect the write within 5 seconds
   163→  And no stale reads occur after the consistency window
   164→```
   165→
   166→### Property Criteria from JTBD
   167→
   168→Outcome statements often translate to property-shaped criteria (ongoing qualities):
   169→
   170→| Outcome Statement | Property Scenario |
   171→|-------------------|-------------------|
   172→| "Minimize the likelihood of undetected failures" | `@property` monitoring coverage: all critical paths emit health signals |
   173→| "Minimize the time to recover from exceptions" | `@property` recovery time: system returns to healthy state within 60s |
   174→| "Maximize the likelihood that results are consistent" | `@property` consistency: identical inputs produce identical outputs |
   175→
   176→## JTBD-to-BDD Translation Template
   177→
   178→Use when translating a complete job story into a BDD scenario set:
   179→
   180→```markdown
   181→## JTBD-to-BDD Translation: [Feature Name]
   182→
   183→### Source Job Story
   184→When [situation],
   185→I want to [motivation],
   186→so I can [outcome].
   187→
   188→### Forces
   189→- Push: [frustration]
   190→- Pull: [attraction]
   191→- Anxiety: [fear]
   192→- Habit: [inertia]
   193→
   194→### Scenarios
   195→
   196→#### Happy Path (from job story)
   197→Scenario: [outcome achieved]
   198→  Given [situation]
   199→  When [action aligned with motivation]
   200→  Then [outcome measurable]
   201→
   202→#### Anxiety Path (from forces)
   203→Scenario: [fear addressed]
   204→  Given [anxiety-triggering context]
   205→  When [fear event occurs]
   206→  Then [safety net / reassurance]
   207→
   208→#### Habit Path (from forces)
   209→Scenario: [transition supported]
   210→  Given [user accustomed to old way]
   211→  When [new approach encountered]
   212→  Then [familiar patterns preserved]
   213→
   214→#### Job Map Edge Cases
   215→Scenario: [Define step gap]
   216→  Given [insufficient information]
   217→  When [user attempts to start]
   218→  Then [system guides to missing info]
   219→
   220→Scenario: [Monitor step gap]
   221→  Given [misleading success indicators]
   222→  When [user checks results]
   223→  Then [system surfaces hidden issues]
   224→
   225→#### Properties (from outcome statements)
   226→@property
   227→Scenario: [ongoing quality]
   228→  Given [system under normal operation]
   229→  Then [quality metric holds within threshold]
   230→```
   231→
   232→## Integration Checklist
   233→
   234→Before handing scenarios to acceptance-designer (DISTILL wave), verify:
   235→
   236→- [ ] Happy path scenario derived from job story
   237→- [ ] Anxiety scenario addresses strongest demand-reducing force
   238→- [ ] Habit scenario supports users transitioning from current workflow
   239→- [ ] At least 4 of 8 job map steps have dedicated edge case scenarios
   240→- [ ] Property-shaped criteria tagged with @property
   241→- [ ] All scenarios use concrete data (real names, real values)
   242→- [ ] Scenarios are solution-neutral where possible (describe outcomes, not implementation)
   243→
   244→## Cross-References
   245→
