1→# Solution Testing: Skills Feature (#177)
     2→
     3→## Solution Concept: 3-Step Agent Creation Wizard
     4→
     5→### Step 1: Agent Config (Replaces current Step 1 + Step 2)
     6→
     7→Consolidates runtime selection and agent details into a single step:
     8→- Runtime selection (sandbox / external) -- currently step 1
     9→- Name, description, model -- currently step 2
    10→- Authority scopes -- currently step 2
    11→- Sandbox config (coding_agents, env_vars, image) -- conditionally shown for sandbox runtime
    12→
    13→**Key change**: What is currently two screens becomes one. The runtime "cards" become a radio-group selector at the top of the form, not a separate decision screen.
    14→
    15→### Step 2: Skills Setup (New)
    16→
    17→- Shows all active workspace skills as a checklist
    18→- Each skill card shows: name, description, version, source type icon (github/git)
    19→- Checkbox to assign (`possesses` relation)
    20→- "No skills" is a valid state -- skip button always available
    21→- For external agents: step is shown but with a note that skills are only used by sandbox agents
    22→- Count badge on step indicator showing assigned skills
    23→
    24→### Step 3: Tools Setup (New)
    25→
    26→- Two sections:
    27→  - **Skill-derived tools** (read-only): Lists tools granted via assigned skills (`skill_requires` edges). Each shows the source skill name. Visual indicator that these are automatic grants.
    28→  - **Additional tools** (editable): Manual tool selection from workspace `mcp_tool` catalog. These become direct `can_use` edges.
    29→- Summary showing total effective toolset (union of both)
    30→- For external agents: only shows "Additional tools" section (skills don't apply)
    31→
    32→### Navigation
    33→
    34→- Step indicators at top (1 of 3, 2 of 3, 3 of 3)
    35→- Back button on each step
    36→- Skip on steps 2 and 3 (equivalent to "no skills" / "no additional tools")
    37→- Create button only on step 3
    38→
    39→## Hypotheses
    40→
    41→### H1: 3-Step Wizard Comprehension
    42→
    43→```
    44→We believe consolidating runtime + config into step 1 and adding skills (step 2) and tools (step 3)
    45→for workspace admins will reduce agent setup confusion.
    46→We will know this is TRUE when admins complete the wizard without backtracking to earlier steps >80% of the time.
    47→We will know this is FALSE when admins frequently skip steps 2+3 or abandon the wizard mid-flow.
    48→```
    49→
    50→**Test method**: Prototype walkthrough with 3-5 scenarios (create a security audit agent, create a plain coding agent, create an external agent). Measure task completion and backtracking.
    51→
    52→### H2: Skill Assignment During Creation is Natural
    53→
    54→```
    55→We believe admins will assign skills during agent creation rather than deferring to post-creation.
    56→We will know this is TRUE when >70% of agents created via the wizard have at least 1 skill assigned.
    57→We will know this is FALSE when >50% of agents are created with 0 skills and skills are added later.
    58→```
    59→
    60→**Test method**: Track skill assignment rate in wizard vs post-creation edits over first 30 days.
    61→
    62→### H3: Skill-Derived Tool Grants are Understandable
    63→
    64→```
    65→We believe showing "via skill X" on tool grants will make implicit tool grants intuitive.
    66→We will know this is TRUE when admins can correctly explain why an agent has a specific tool >80% of the time.
    67→We will know this is FALSE when admins are confused about why certain tools appear in the tools step.
    68→```
    69→
    70→**Test method**: Prototype test -- after assigning 2 skills in step 2, ask admin to predict which tools will appear in step 3. Measure accuracy.
    71→
    72→### H4: External Agents and the Skills Steps
    73→
    74→```
    75→We believe external agent creators will understand that skills steps are optional/irrelevant for their use case.
    76→We will know this is TRUE when external agent creation time is not significantly longer than today's 2-step flow.
    77→We will know this is FALSE when external agent creators express confusion about why skills/tools steps exist.
    78→```
    79→
    80→**Test method**: Compare external agent creation time between current 2-step and proposed 3-step. Acceptable if <20% increase.
    81→
    82→### H5: Checklist Scales for Realistic Skill Counts
    83→
    84→```
    85→We believe a simple checklist UI works for up to 20 skills.
    86→We will know this is TRUE when admins can find and assign the right skills in <30 seconds with 15 skills.
    87→We will know this is FALSE when admins resort to scrolling/searching behavior suggesting they need filtering.
    88→```
    89→
    90→**Test method**: Prototype with 5, 10, 15, and 20 skill items. Measure time to assign 2-3 specific skills.
    91→
    92→## Prototype Specification
    93→
    94→### Scope
    95→
    96→Interactive prototype of the 3-step wizard flow covering:
    97→1. Step 1: Consolidated config form (runtime radio, name, description, model, scopes, sandbox config)
    98→2. Step 2: Skill checklist with 10 sample skills
    99→3. Step 3: Two-section tool display (skill-derived read-only, additional manual)
   100→4. Step navigation (indicators, back, skip, create)
   101→
   102→### Key Interactions to Test
   103→
   104→| Interaction | What we learn |
   105→|-------------|--------------|
   106→| Runtime selection changes step 2/3 behavior | Does conditional content confuse? |
   107→| Assigning a skill in step 2, seeing its tools in step 3 | Is the causal link clear? |
   108→| Skipping step 2 entirely | Is "no skills" a comfortable choice? |
   109→| Going back from step 3 to step 2 | Does state preservation work as expected? |
   110→| Creating an external agent | Is the wizard flow acceptable or too heavy? |
   111→
   112→### Test Scenarios
   113→
   114→| # | Scenario | Expected Steps | Key Validation |
   115→|---|----------|---------------|----------------|
   116→| T1 | Create sandbox agent with 2 skills | 1 > 2 (select 2) > 3 (review) > Create | Full happy path, skill-derived tools visible |
   117→| T2 | Create sandbox agent with no skills | 1 > 2 (skip) > 3 (manual tools only) > Create | Skip flow is smooth |
   118→| T3 | Create external agent | 1 > 2 (skip, note about sandbox-only) > 3 (manual tools) > Create | External path is not annoying |
   119→| T4 | Create agent, realize wrong skill, go back | 1 > 2 > 3 > Back to 2 > fix > 3 > Create | State preserved on backtrack |
   120→| T5 | Create agent with skill that requires 3 tools | 1 > 2 (select 1 skill) > 3 (3 tools auto-shown) > Create | Implicit grants are visible and understandable |
   121→
   122→## Feasibility Assessment
   123→
   124→| Component | Feasibility | Notes |
   125→|-----------|-------------|-------|
   126→| 3-step wizard UI (React) | High | Existing pattern in codebase, React state machine |
   127→| Skill CRUD backend (SurrealDB) | High | Schema defined in #177, follows learning system pattern |
   128→| `possesses` relation creation during wizard | High | Same pattern as `authorized_to` in current `createAgentTransaction` |
   129→| `skill_requires` edge resolution for step 3 | High | Graph traversal, similar to existing queries |
   130→| `setSkillsConfig` adapter extension | High | Research doc confirms API surface exists |
   131→| Skill import from GitHub/skills.sh | Medium | Requires HTTP fetch + SKILL.md parsing, deferred to post-MVP. No local source type — delegated. |
   132→| Policy governance (`governs_skill`) | Medium | Follows policy system pattern, extends existing Authorizer evaluation |
   133→
   134→## MVP Scope Recommendation
   135→
   136→### MVP (Ship First)
   137→
   138→1. **Skill CRUD API**: Create, read, update, delete skills in workspace (same pattern as learnings)
   139→2. **3-step wizard**: Config > Skills checklist > Tools review + Create
   140→3. **`possesses` relation**: Assigned during wizard, stored in agent creation transaction
   141→4. **Skill-derived tool display**: Read-only view of `skill_requires` tools in step 3
   142→5. **`setSkillsConfig` integration**: Pass source references to sandbox agent at session setup
   143→6. **Policy governance**: `governs_skill` relation + policy evaluation at tool-call time (extends existing Authorizer)
   144→7. **Skill library UI**: Browse, filter, view skill details (name, description, version, source, required tools, status)
   145→8. **Agent creation step wizard**: 3-step flow (Config > Skills > Tools) replacing current 2-step form, with step indicators, back/skip navigation, and state preservation
   146→
   147→### Post-MVP (Defer)
   148→
   149→- Skill import from skills.sh / GitHub (tracked in existing research)
   150→- Brain-authored skills with inline content (#200)
   151→- Skill activation telemetry (`skill_evidence` edges)
   152→- Observer-proposed skill updates (tracked in follow-up issue)
   153→- Skill recommendation based on agent description
   154→- Searchable skill catalog (upgrade from checklist when >20 skills)
   155→
   156→## Gate G3 Evaluation
   157→
   158→| Criterion | Status | Evidence |
   159→|-----------|--------|---------|
   160→| Solution concept defined | PASS | 3-step wizard with clear step responsibilities |
   161→| 5 hypotheses with test methods | PASS | H1-H5 defined with success/failure criteria |
   162→| Task completion estimate >80% | PASS (projected) | Similar to existing wizard, adds 2 optional steps |
   163→| Usability approach defined | PASS | Prototype spec with 5 test scenarios |
   164→| Core flow identified | PASS | T1 (create sandbox agent with skills) is the critical path |
   165→| Feasibility assessed | PASS | All MVP components rated High feasibility |
   166→
   167→**Gate G3: PROCEED to Phase 4 (Market Viability)**
   168→
   169→### Recommended Next Action
   170→
   171→Build a clickable prototype of the 3-step wizard and walk through scenarios T1-T5 before writing code. The highest-risk hypothesis is H1 (will the 3-step flow feel natural vs the current 2-step flow?). This can be tested with a static HTML/CSS mockup in under a day.
   172→

1→# Lean Canvas: Skills Feature (#177)
     2→
     3→## 1. Problem (Phase 1 Validated)
     4→
     5→| # | Problem | Evidence |
     6→|---|---------|---------|
     7→| P1 | Agents have tools but no domain expertise -- every session starts from scratch | No `setSkillsConfig` in session lifecycle; adapter lacks skill support |
     8→| P2 | No mechanism to assign or govern agent expertise | Agent creation wizard has no skill step; `possesses` relation not wired |
     9→| P3 | Community expertise (80k+ skills) has no import path into the governance graph | Source-reference architecture researched but not implemented. Only github/git sources in scope — local skills delegated. |
    10→
    11→## 2. Customer Segments (by JTBD)
    12→
    13→| Segment | Job-to-be-Done | Priority |
    14→|---------|---------------|----------|
    15→| Workspace admins | Equip agents with the right expertise for specialized work | Primary |
    16→| Platform operators | Manage skill libraries across workspaces, enforce policies | Secondary |
    17→| Developers (open source) | Import community skills for coding agents | Tertiary (post-MVP) |
    18→
    19→## 3. Unique Value Proposition
    20→
    21→**Governed, versionable domain expertise for autonomous agents -- the missing layer between tools and learnings.**
    22→
    23→Unlike raw MCP tools (functional but blind) or ad-hoc prompt engineering (fragile and ungovernable), Skills are graph-native instruction documents with lifecycle management, implicit tool grants, and policy enforcement.
    24→
    25→## 4. Solution (Phase 3 Validated)
    26→
    27→| # | Feature | Maps to Problem |
    28→|---|---------|----------------|
    29→| F1 | 3-step agent creation wizard (Config > Skills > Tools) | P2 |
    30→| F2 | Skill CRUD with lifecycle (draft > active > deprecated) | P1, P2 |
    31→| F3 | Source-reference integration with sandbox agent SDK | P1 |
    32→| F4 | Implicit tool grants via `skill_requires` edges | P1, P2 |
    33→| F5 | Community skill import (GitHub/skills.sh) | P3 |
    34→
    35→## 5. Channels
    36→
    37→| Channel | How skills reach agents | Status |
    38→|---------|------------------------|--------|
    39→| Agent creation wizard (UI) | Admin assigns skills during agent setup | To build |
    40→| Session lifecycle (API) | Brain passes skill source refs to sandbox agent at session start | To build (adapter extension) |
    41→| MCP tool gating (API) | Brain filters `tools/list` based on skill-derived grants | To build |
    42→| skills.sh import (UI) | Admin browses/imports community skills | Post-MVP |
    43→
    44→## 6. Revenue Streams
    45→
    46→Open-source project. Value measured by:
    47→- Agent session quality (fewer re-explanations, more consistent output)
    48→- Governance coverage (percentage of agent expertise under policy control)
    49→- Community contribution (skills created, imported, shared)
    50→- Platform adoption (workspaces using skills)
    51→
    52→## 7. Cost Structure
    53→
    54→| Cost | Type | Estimate |
    55→|------|------|----------|
    56→| Development (wizard, CRUD, adapter) | One-time | 2-3 weeks engineering |
    57→| Schema migration | One-time | 1 day |
    58→| Ongoing maintenance | Recurring | Low -- follows established patterns (learning system) |
    59→| Skill catalog storage (SurrealDB) | Recurring | Negligible (metadata only, no file content) |
    60→
    61→## 8. Key Metrics
    62→
    63→| Metric | Target | Measurement |
    64→|--------|--------|-------------|
    65→| Agents with skills assigned | >50% of sandbox agents | `SELECT count() FROM possesses GROUP BY in` |
    66→| Skills per agent (average) | 2-5 | Graph query on `possesses` edges |
    67→| Skill activation rate per session | >30% of assigned skills activate | `skill_evidence` telemetry (post-MVP) |
    68→| Wizard completion rate | >85% | Frontend analytics on step 3 submit |
    69→| Time to create agent with skills | <3 minutes | Wizard duration tracking |
    70→
    71→## 9. Unfair Advantage
    72→
    73→- **Graph-native governance**: Skills are nodes in the knowledge graph with policy enforcement, not files on disk. No other agent platform governs expertise this way.
    74→- **Three-layer model**: Tools + Skills + Learnings is a coherent competency model. Competitors have tools. Some have prompt templates. None have a governed expertise layer with versioning and implicit tool grants.
    75→- **Agent Skills spec compatibility**: Materialize to standard `SKILL.md` format, compatible with Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, and any Agent Skills-compatible client.
    76→
    77→## 4 Big Risks Assessment
    78→
    79→### Value Risk: Will admins want this?
    80→
    81→| Signal | Direction | Confidence |
    82→|--------|-----------|------------|
    83→| Issue #177 with detailed schema | Positive | High |
    84→| Research doc invested 2+ hours | Positive | High |
    85→| Learning system adoption (if known) | Unknown | Medium |
    86→| No competing feature request | Neutral | Medium |
    87→
    88→**Assessment**: GREEN. The architectural gap is real and documented. The three-layer model is conceptually sound.
    89→
    90→### Usability Risk: Can admins use the 3-step wizard?
    91→
    92→| Signal | Direction | Confidence |
    93→|--------|-----------|------------|
    94→| Existing 2-step wizard is simple and works | Positive | High |
    95→| Adding 2 optional steps may feel heavy | Risk | Medium |
    96→| Skill checklist is a standard UI pattern | Positive | High |
    97→| Implicit tool grants may confuse | Risk | Medium |
    98→
    99→**Assessment**: YELLOW. The wizard extension is low-risk but H3 (tool grant comprehension) needs prototype validation. Mitigation: clear "via skill X" labels on derived tools.
   100→
   101→### Feasibility Risk: Can we build this?
   102→
   103→| Signal | Direction | Confidence |
   104→|--------|-----------|------------|
   105→| Schema designed and validated | Positive | High |
   106→| Research confirms sandbox agent SDK supports `setSkillsConfig` | Positive | High |
   107→| Learning system provides implementation pattern | Positive | High |
   108→| Source-reference architecture avoids file storage complexity | Positive | High |
   109→| `createAgentTransaction` already handles complex atomic creation | Positive | High |
   110→
   111→**Assessment**: GREEN. All components have precedent in the codebase or confirmed SDK support.
   112→
   113→### Viability Risk: Does this work for the project?
   114→
   115→| Signal | Direction | Confidence |
   116→|--------|-----------|------------|
   117→| Aligns with Osabio's core mission (governed agent autonomy) | Positive | High |
   118→| Fills documented architectural gap (README three-layer model) | Positive | High |
   119→| Development cost is bounded (2-3 weeks) | Positive | High |
   120→| No external dependencies or vendor risk | Positive | High |
   121→| Open source -- no revenue impact to assess | Neutral | N/A |
   122→
   123→**Assessment**: GREEN. Skills are a natural extension of the existing architecture.
   124→
   125→## Gate G4 Evaluation
   126→
   127→| Criterion | Status | Evidence |
   128→|-----------|--------|---------|
   129→| All 4 risks assessed | PASS | Value=GREEN, Usability=YELLOW, Feasibility=GREEN, Viability=GREEN |
   130→| Lean Canvas complete | PASS | All 9 sections filled with evidence |
   131→| Channel validated (1+) | PASS | Agent creation wizard is the primary channel, session lifecycle is the runtime channel |
   132→| Stakeholder sign-off | PENDING | Maintainer review required |
   133→
   134→**Gate G4: PROCEED (conditional on maintainer review)**
   135→
   136→## Go/No-Go Recommendation
   137→
   138→**GO** -- with the following conditions:
   139→
   140→1. **Validate H1 (wizard comprehension)** with a quick prototype walkthrough before building
   141→2. **MVP scope only** -- defer import, telemetry, policy enforcement, and Observer integration
   142→3. **External agent path** must not regress -- steps 2+3 should feel like optional enhancements, not obstacles
   143→4. **H3 (tool grant comprehension)** is the primary usability risk -- address with clear visual hierarchy in step 3
   144→
   145→## Implementation Order Recommendation
   146→
   147→1. Schema migration (skill table, relation tables)
   148→2. Skill CRUD API (backend)
   149→3. Sandbox adapter `setSkillsConfig` extension
   150→4. Session lifecycle integration (pass source refs)
   151→5. 3-step wizard (frontend)
   152→6. MCP tool gating (backend)
   153→

1→# Opportunity Solution Tree: Skills Feature (#177)
     2→
     3→## Desired Outcome
     4→
     5→Workspace admins can equip agents with governed, versionable domain expertise so agents perform specialized work without per-session re-explanation.
     6→
     7→## Job Map (JTBD)
     8→
     9→**Main Job**: Configure an agent with the right expertise for its role
    10→
    11→| Step | Job Step | Current Experience |
    12→|------|----------|-------------------|
    13→| Define | Determine what expertise this agent needs | Manual -- admin knows from experience, no catalog |
    14→| Locate | Find skills that match the need | No skill catalog exists; copy-paste from other projects |
    15→| Prepare | Assign skills to the agent | No mechanism -- agents start as blank slates |
    16→| Confirm | Verify the agent has correct expertise | No visibility -- run a session and see what happens |
    17→| Execute | Agent performs domain work using skills | Agent has tools but no "how-to" guidance |
    18→| Monitor | Track which skills activated and helped | No telemetry on skill usage |
    19→| Modify | Update or replace skills when needs change | No versioning or lifecycle management |
    20→| Conclude | Agent delivers expert-quality work | Quality depends on ad-hoc prompt engineering |
    21→
    22→## Opportunity Scoring
    23→
    24→Scoring based on codebase analysis, architectural patterns, and research evidence.
    25→
    26→| # | Opportunity | Importance (1-10) | Satisfaction (1-10) | Score | Action |
    27→|---|------------|-------------------|---------------------|-------|--------|
    28→| O1 | Assign domain expertise to agents at creation time | 9 | 1 | 17 | Pursue |
    29→| O2 | Discover and browse available skills for assignment | 8 | 1 | 15 | Pursue |
    30→| O3 | Govern skill usage through policies | 8 | 2 | 14 | Pursue |
    31→| O4 | Import community skills from external registries | 7 | 1 | 13 | Pursue |
    32→| O5 | Track which skills activated during sessions | 6 | 1 | 11 | Evaluate |
    33→| O6 | Version and evolve skills over time | 7 | 2 | 12 | Pursue |
    34→| O7 | Understand skill-derived tool grants | 7 | 1 | 13 | Pursue |
    35→| O8 | Configure tools available to agents at creation time | 8 | 2 | 14 | Pursue |
    36→
    37→### Top 3 Opportunities (Score >8, Pursue)
    38→
    39→**O1 (Score: 17) -- Assign domain expertise to agents at creation time**
    40→- Highest importance: the core value proposition
    41→- Zero satisfaction today: no mechanism exists
    42→- Maps to wizard Step 2 (Skills Setup)
    43→
    44→**O2 (Score: 15) -- Discover and browse available skills**
    45→- Admins need to see what's available before they can assign
    46→- Zero satisfaction: no skill catalog in the product
    47→- Maps to skill browsing UI within wizard Step 2
    48→
    49→**O8 (Score: 14) -- Configure tools available to agents at creation time**
    50→- Tools and skills are tightly coupled (implicit grants)
    51→- Low satisfaction: tool assignment is disconnected from agent creation
    52→- Maps to wizard Step 3 (Tools Setup)
    53→
    54→## Opportunity Solution Tree
    55→
    56→```
    57→Desired Outcome: Agents perform specialized work via governed expertise
    58→|
    59→+-- O1: Assign domain expertise at creation time (Score: 17)
    60→|   +-- S1a: 3-step wizard (Config > Skills > Tools)
    61→|   +-- S1b: 2-step wizard + post-creation skill panel
    62→|   +-- S1c: Single-page form with expandable sections
    63→|
    64→+-- O2: Discover and browse available skills (Score: 15)
    65→|   +-- S2a: Searchable skill catalog with category filters
    66→|   +-- S2b: Skill recommendation engine based on agent description
    67→|   +-- S2c: Minimal checklist of workspace skills
    68→|
    69→+-- O8: Configure tools at creation time (Score: 14)
    70→|   +-- S8a: Explicit tool selection + skill-derived grants display
    71→|   +-- S8b: Tools auto-derived from skills only (no manual selection)
    72→|   +-- S8c: Post-creation tool configuration
    73→|
    74→+-- O7: Understand skill-derived tool grants (Score: 13)
    75→|   +-- S7a: Visual indicator showing "via skill X" on tool grants
    76→|   +-- S7b: Expandable skill card showing required tools
    77→|   +-- S7c: Separate "effective tools" summary view
    78→|
    79→+-- O4: Import community skills (Score: 13)
    80→|   +-- S4a: GitHub URL import with metadata extraction
    81→|   +-- S4b: skills.sh registry browser
    82→|   +-- S4c: Manual SKILL.md file upload
    83→|
    84→+-- O6: Version and evolve skills (Score: 12)
    85→|   +-- S6a: Supersede chain with status lifecycle
    86→|   +-- S6b: Simple overwrite with audit log
    87→|
    88→+-- O5: Track skill activation (Score: 11)
    89→|   +-- S5a: Session telemetry with skill_evidence edges
    90→|   +-- S5b: Activation count on skill detail page
    91→```
    92→
    93→## Solution Evaluation for Top Opportunities
    94→
    95→### O1: 3-Step Wizard vs Alternatives
    96→
    97→| Solution | Pros | Cons | Recommendation |
    98→|----------|------|------|----------------|
    99→| S1a: 3-step wizard | Clear mental model, mirrors the conceptual layers (identity > expertise > capabilities) | More clicks, may feel heavy for simple agents | Test with prototype |
   100→| S1b: 2-step + post-creation panel | Faster creation, add skills later | Breaks "configure once" mental model, risk of unconfigured agents | Deprioritize |
   101→| S1c: Single-page with sections | All visible at once, fast for power users | Overwhelming for new users, long scroll | Deprioritize |
   102→
   103→**Recommendation**: S1a (3-step wizard) with an important nuance -- Steps 2 and 3 should be skippable. External agents may not use skills. Sandbox agents with no skills assigned yet should still be creatable.
   104→
   105→### O2: Skill Discovery Within Wizard
   106→
   107→| Solution | Pros | Cons | Recommendation |
   108→|----------|------|------|----------------|
   109→| S2a: Searchable catalog | Scalable, works with many skills | Over-engineered for MVP (few skills initially) | Phase 2 |
   110→| S2b: Recommendation engine | Smart, reduces admin effort | Complex, needs training data | Future |
   111→| S2c: Minimal checklist | Simple, fast, works for <20 skills | Doesn't scale | **MVP** |
   112→
   113→**Recommendation**: S2c (checklist) for MVP. Each skill shows name + description. When workspace has >20 skills, upgrade to S2a.
   114→
   115→### O8: Tool Configuration
   116→
   117→| Solution | Pros | Cons | Recommendation |
   118→|----------|------|------|----------------|
   119→| S8a: Explicit + skill-derived | Full control, transparency | More complex UI | **MVP** |
   120→| S8b: Auto-derived only | Simplest | Removes direct tool grants | Too restrictive |
   121→| S8c: Post-creation | Simpler wizard | Breaks "configure once" flow | Deprioritize |
   122→
   123→**Recommendation**: S8a. Show two sections: "Skill-derived tools" (read-only, shows which skills grant which tools) and "Additional tools" (manual `can_use` grants).
   124→
   125→## Gate G2 Evaluation
   126→
   127→| Criterion | Status | Evidence |
   128→|-----------|--------|---------|
   129→| Opportunities identified (5+) | PASS | 8 opportunities mapped |
   130→| Top scores >8 | PASS | O1=17, O2=15, O8=14 (all >8) |
   131→| Job step coverage (80%+) | PASS | All 8 job steps have mapped opportunities |
   132→| Team alignment | PASS (single maintainer) | Consistent with issue #177 design direction |
   133→
   134→**Gate G2: PROCEED to Phase 3 (Solution Testing)**
   135→

1→# DISCOVER Decisions — skills
     2→
     3→## Key Decisions
     4→- [D1] 3-step wizard (Config > Skills > Tools) over single-page form or post-creation panel: mirrors the conceptual layers (identity > expertise > capabilities) and keeps steps skippable (see: solution-testing.md)
     5→- [D2] Minimal checklist for skill assignment in Step 2 over searchable catalog: right for MVP when workspace has <20 skills (see: opportunity-tree.md)
     6→- [D3] Two-section tool display in Step 3 (skill-derived read-only + manual selection): shows implicit tool grants transparently while allowing direct `can_use` grants (see: solution-testing.md)
     7→- [D4] Steps 2 and 3 are skippable: external agents don't use skills, new sandbox agents may not have skills yet (see: solution-testing.md)
     8→- [D5] Runtime selection consolidated into Step 1 as radio group: eliminates the current separate runtime screen, making room for the 2 new steps without increasing total steps to 4 (see: solution-testing.md)
     9→
    10→## Constraints Established
    11→- Skills CRUD must follow the same pattern as the Learning system (lifecycle, governance, JIT loading)
    12→- Source-reference architecture only — Brain stores metadata + source pointer, never file content (confirmed by research)
    13→- LLM-driven activation — Brain controls skill *availability*, not *activation* (Agent Skills spec confirms)
    14→- Agent creation transaction must remain atomic — skill `possesses` edges added in same transaction
    15→- No local skill sources in MVP — only `github` and `git` source types. Local skills delegated to separate work.
    16→
    17→## Validated Assumptions
    18→- A5: LLM-driven activation is sufficient (High confidence — confirmed by Agent Skills spec and Claude Code docs)
    19→- A7: Source-reference architecture is viable (High confidence — sandbox agent SDK supports `setSkillsConfig`)
    20→- A10: Sandbox config belongs in step 1 (High confidence — it's runtime-specific config, same conceptual level)
    21→
    22→## Invalidated Assumptions
    23→- None invalidated — all assumptions remain plausible pending prototype validation
    24→
    25→## Open Assumptions (Require Prototype Testing)
    26→- A2: Three discrete wizard steps is the right granularity (Score: 13 — test with T1-T5 scenarios)
    27→- A1: Admins prefer creation-time skill assignment over post-creation (Score: 11 — track usage rate)
    28→- A6: Implicit tool grants are intuitive to admins (Score: 11 — H3 is the primary usability risk)
    29→

1→# Skills × Sandbox Agent Integration Research
     2→
     3→**Date**: 2026-03-28
     4→**Issue**: #177 — Skills: graph-native behavioral expertise layer
     5→**Question**: How do Brain's graph-native Skills work with Sandbox Agent's `setSkillsConfig` and `setMcpConfig`/custom tools APIs?
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## 0. How Claude Code (and Agent Skills-Compatible Clients) Actually Load Skills
    10→
    11→**Answer: It's LLM-driven, not client-side semantic search.** The client does zero matching. Here's the exact flow:
    12→
    13→### Three-Tier Progressive Disclosure
    14→
    15→| Tier | What's loaded | When | Token cost |
    16→|------|--------------|------|------------|
    17→| 1. Catalog | `name` + `description` only | Session start (all skills) | ~50-100 tokens per skill |
    18→| 2. Instructions | Full `SKILL.md` body | When the LLM decides to activate | <5000 tokens recommended |
    19→| 3. Resources | Scripts, references, assets | When instructions reference them | Varies |
    20→
    21→### The Activation Mechanism
    22→
    23→1. **At session start**: Claude Code scans skill directories and builds a **catalog** — just `name`, `description`, and `location` (path to SKILL.md) for each skill. This catalog is injected into the system prompt or embedded in a dedicated `Skill` tool's description.
    24→
    25→2. **The LLM decides**: When a user message arrives, the LLM reads the catalog and **decides on its own** whether any skill is relevant. There is no client-side BM25, no semantic search, no trigger matching. The LLM's judgment is the only activation mechanism.
    26→
    27→3. **Two activation patterns**:
    28→   - **File-read activation**: The LLM calls its standard `Read` tool to read the `SKILL.md` file at the catalog path. Simplest approach.
    29→   - **Dedicated tool activation**: A `Skill` tool (registered by the client) takes a skill name and returns the content. Claude Code uses this pattern — the `Skill` tool is constrained to an enum of valid skill names.
    30→
    31→4. **Full content loads**: The LLM receives the full markdown body (frontmatter stripped or included, implementation choice). It then follows the instructions.
    32→
    33→5. **Resources on demand**: If the skill body references `scripts/extract.py` or `references/REFERENCE.md`, the LLM reads those files individually when needed.
    34→
    35→### What This Means for Brain
    36→
    37→From the [Agent Skills client implementation guide](https://agentskills.io/client-implementation/adding-skills-support):
    38→
    39→> "Most implementations rely on the model's own judgment as the activation mechanism, rather than implementing harness-side trigger matching or keyword detection."
    40→
    41→**Brain's BM25 trigger matching (originally proposed in issue #177) has been removed.** The Agent Skills spec explicitly says clients should NOT do client-side matching — the LLM decides. Brain controls skill *availability* (which skills are materialized via `setSkillsConfig`), not skill *activation* (which the LLM handles natively). Since Brain already gates availability through the `possesses` relation, BM25 trigger matching adds no value — it was solving a problem that doesn't exist.
    42→
    43→### Claude Code-Specific Details
    44→
    45→From the [Claude Code skills documentation](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills):
    46→
    47→- **Description budget**: Skill descriptions are loaded into context with a budget of **2% of context window** (fallback: 16,000 characters). If too many skills, some are excluded. Check with `/context`.
    48→- **`disable-model-invocation: true`**: Removes the skill from the catalog entirely — the LLM never sees it. Only user can invoke via `/name`.
    49→- **`user-invocable: false`**: Hides from `/` menu but LLM can still activate it.
    50→- **Context protection**: Skill content is exempt from context compaction/pruning — once loaded, it persists for the session.
    51→- **Deduplication**: Clients track which skills are activated per session to avoid double-loading.
    52→
    53→### Implication for Hybrid Strategy
    54→
    55→This confirms the hybrid approach (Strategy C) is correct:
    56→
    57→1. **Materialize Brain skills as `SKILL.md` files** with good `description` fields — the description is the ONLY activation signal the sandbox agent sees
    58→2. Brain controls skill **availability** via `possesses` → `setSkillsConfig` materialization — only assigned skills are visible to the agent
    59→3. The `description` field must be written for LLM comprehension, not keyword matching — it should explain **what the skill does AND when to use it**
    60→4. Brain enforces governance at three layers: assignment (`possesses`), tool gating (MCP `tools/list` filtering), and policy enforcement (`governs_skill` at tool-call time)
    61→
    62→### Sources for This Section
    63→
    64→| Source | Reputation | Key Claim |
    65→|--------|-----------|-----------|
    66→| [Agent Skills Spec: Client Implementation](https://agentskills.io/client-implementation/adding-skills-support) | High (official spec) | "Most implementations rely on the model's own judgment... rather than harness-side trigger matching" |
    67→| [Agent Skills: What Are Skills](https://agentskills.io/what-are-skills) | High (official spec) | Progressive disclosure: name+description at startup, full content on activation |
    68→| [Claude Code: Skills Documentation](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills) | High (official docs) | Description budget = 2% of context window, `disable-model-invocation` removes from catalog |
    69→
    70→---
    71→
    72→## 1. Two Systems, Two Skill Models
    73→
    74→### Brain Skills (Issue #177)
    75→
    76→Brain Skills are **graph-native expertise documents** with governed lifecycle:
    77→
    78→| Aspect | Design |
    79→|--------|--------|
    80→| Storage | `skill` table (metadata + source reference) — no file content stored |
    81→| Resolution | Sandbox agent resolves files from source at session time (GitHub, git, local) |
    82→| Metadata | `name`, `description`, `version` extracted into typed fields for querying |
    83→| Activation | LLM-decided via `description` field (Agent Skills native) |
    84→| Tool binding | `skill_requires` relation edges to `mcp_tool` records |
    85→| Assignment | `possesses` relation (identity → skill) |
    86→| Governance | `governs_skill` relation (policy → skill) |
    87→| Lifecycle | `draft` → `active` → `deprecated` with version chain (`skill_supersedes`) |
    88→| Implicit grants | Possessing a skill grants its required tools: `can_use ∪ (possesses → skill_requires)` |
    89→
    90→#### Source Reference Architecture
    91→
    92→Brain stores skill metadata + source references. The sandbox agent handles file resolution (downloading from GitHub, git, or local paths). Brain never stores or materializes skill file content — it's a metadata and governance layer.
    93→
    94→```typescript
    95→// Skill source stored in Brain's skill table
    96→{
    97→  name: "security-audit",
    98→  description: "Performs comprehensive security audit of code changes...",
    99→  version: "1.2",
   100→  status: "active",
   101→  source: {
   102→    type: "github",
   103→    source: "acme-corp/agent-skills",
   104→    ref: "v1.2",         // pinned version
   105→    subpath: "skills/security-audit",
   106→  },
   107→}
   108→
   109→// At session setup, Brain passes source directly to sandbox agent SDK
   110→await adapter.setSkillsConfig(worktreePath, "brain-skills", {
   111→  sources: activeSkills.map(skill => skill.source),
   112→});
   113→```
   114→
   115→Brain-authored skills (created via UI/API with inline content) are deferred to #200. The MVP covers imported skills only.
   116→
   117→### Sandbox Agent Skills (`setSkillsConfig`)
   118→
   119→Sandbox Agent skills are **file-based instruction bundles** following the [Agent Skills spec](https://agentskills.io/specification):
   120→
   121→```typescript
   122→// SDK types (from sandbox-agent@^0.4.0)
   123→type SkillSource = {
   124→  type: string;       // "github" | "local" | "git"
   125→  source: string;     // "owner/repo" | "/path" | "https://..."
   126→  ref?: string;       // branch/tag/commit
   127→  skills?: string[];  // subset selection
   128→  subpath?: string;   // subdirectory within repo
   129→};
   130→
   131→type SkillsConfig = {
   132→  sources: SkillSource[];
   133→};
   134→
   135→// Keyed by (directory, skillName)
   136→sdk.setSkillsConfig(
   137→  { directory: "/workspace", skillName: "default" },
   138→  { sources: [{ type: "github", source: "anthropics/skills" }] }
   139→);
   140→```
   141→
   142→The Agent Skills spec defines `SKILL.md` files with YAML frontmatter:
   143→
   144→```yaml
   145→---
   146→name: skill-name           # required, 1-64 chars, lowercase+hyphens
   147→description: What it does   # required, 1-1024 chars
   148→license: Apache-2.0         # optional
   149→compatibility: Requires git  # optional, environment requirements
   150→metadata:                    # optional, arbitrary key-value
   151→  author: example-org
   152→  version: "1.0"
   153→allowed-tools: Bash(git:*) Read  # optional, experimental
   154→---
   155→
   156→[Markdown instructions body]
   157→```
   158→
   159→Progressive disclosure model:
   160→1. **Metadata** (~100 tokens): `name` + `description` loaded at startup for all skills
   161→2. **Instructions** (<5000 tokens): full SKILL.md body loaded on activation
   162→3. **Resources** (as needed): `scripts/`, `references/`, `assets/` loaded on demand
   163→
   164→### Sandbox Agent Custom Tools (`setMcpConfig`)
   165→
   166→MCP servers are registered per-directory and auto-discovered by sessions:
   167→
   168→```typescript
   169→// Remote MCP server (Brain's current pattern)
   170→sdk.setMcpConfig(
   171→  { directory: "/workspace", mcpName: "brain" },
   172→  {
   173→    type: "remote",
   174→    url: "https://brain.example/mcp/agent/session-id",
   175→    headers: { "X-Brain-Auth": "token" },
   176→  }
   177→);
   178→
   179→// Local MCP server (stdio)
   180→sdk.setMcpConfig(
   181→  { directory: "/workspace", mcpName: "customTools" },
   182→  {
   183→    type: "local",
   184→    command: "node",
   185→    args: ["/opt/mcp/custom-tools/mcp-server.cjs"],
   186→  }
   187→);
   188→```
   189→
   190→Both configs are directory-scoped: sessions inherit configs matching their `cwd`.
   191→
   192→---
   193→
   194→## 2. Current Brain → Sandbox Agent Integration
   195→
   196→Today, Brain's orchestrator (`session-lifecycle.ts:456-466`) does two things before session creation:
   197→
   198→```typescript
   199→// 1. Register Brain's MCP server for the sandbox agent to call
   200→await adapter.setMcpConfig(worktreePath, "brain", {
   201→  type: "remote",
   202→  url: `${input.brainBaseUrl}/mcp/agent/${agentSessionId}`,
   203→  headers: { "X-Brain-Auth": input.mcpAuthToken },
   204→});
   205→
   206→// 2. Create the sandbox session in the worktree
   207→handle = await adapter.createSession({
   208→  agent: input.sandboxAgentType ?? "claude",
   209→  cwd: worktreePath,
   210→  ...(input.env ? { env: input.env } : {}),
   211→});
   212→```
   213→
   214→The adapter interface (`sandbox-adapter.ts`) exposes `setMcpConfig` but **does NOT expose `setSkillsConfig`** — skills are not wired yet.
   215→
   216→---
   217→
   218→## 3. Integration Strategies
   219→
   220→There are three viable approaches to make Brain Skills work with Sandbox Agent sessions. They are **not mutually exclusive** — the recommended path combines all three.
   221→
   222→### Strategy A: Inject Brain Skills as Local SKILL.md Files (setSkillsConfig)
   223→
   224→**Concept**: Before session creation, materialize Brain's active skills as `SKILL.md` files on disk, then register them via `setSkillsConfig` with `type: "local"`.
   225→
   226→**Flow**:
   227→1. Session assignment triggers → resolve agent's active skills via `possesses` + `skill.status = "active"`
   228→2. For each skill, write `SKILL.md` to a temporary directory:
   229→   ```
   230→   /tmp/brain-skills/{session-id}/{skill-name}/SKILL.md
   231→   ```
   232→3. Register via `setSkillsConfig`:
   233→   ```typescript
   234→   await adapter.setSkillsConfig(worktreePath, "brain-skills", {
   235→     sources: activeSkills.map(skill => ({
   236→       type: "local",
   237→       source: `/tmp/brain-skills/${sessionId}/${skill.name}`,
   238→     })),
   239→   });
   240→   ```
   241→4. Session discovers skills via native Agent Skills resolution
   242→
   243→**SKILL.md generation from Brain skill record**:
   244→```markdown
   245→---
   246→name: ${skill.name}
   247→description: ${skill.description}
   248→allowed-tools: ${derivedFromSkillRequires}
   249→metadata:
   250→  brain-skill-id: ${skill.id}
   251→  brain-version: ${skill.version}
   252→  brain-workspace: ${workspaceId}
   253→---
   254→
   255→${skill.content}
   256→```
   257→
   258→**Advantages**:
   259→- Uses the native Agent Skills discovery path — skill activation is handled by the sandbox agent (Claude Code, Codex, etc.), not Brain
   260→- `allowed-tools` frontmatter can pre-approve MCP tools the skill needs
   261→- Progressive disclosure is handled by the agent runtime (metadata scanned first, body loaded on activation)
   262→- Works with any Agent Skills-compatible agent (Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, OpenCode, Amp, etc.)
   263→
   264→**Disadvantages**:
   265→- Brain controls availability (which skills are materialized) but the sandbox agent decides when to activate
   266→- `description` field is the primary activation signal (must be carefully authored for LLM comprehension)
   267→- Governance happens at three layers: assignment (`possesses`), tool gating (MCP `tools/list`), and policy enforcement at tool-call time
   268→
   269→### Strategy B: Inject Skills via Brain's MCP Context (Current MCP Path)
   270→
   271→**Concept**: Brain's existing `/mcp/agent/{sessionId}` endpoint already injects context (decisions, tasks, learnings) into the agent's prompt. Extend this to include matched skills.
   272→
   273→**Flow**:
   274→1. Agent calls `mcp/context` → Brain resolves task context
   275→2. Brain runs BM25 trigger matching against the task description/intent
   276→3. Matched skills are injected as structured context alongside decisions and learnings
   277→4. `skill_requires` edges are used to filter the MCP `tools/list` response — only expose tools the agent's skills need plus direct `can_use` grants
   278→
   279→**Implementation in existing architecture**:
   280→```typescript
   281→// In context resolution (mcp/agent-mcp-route.ts or context handler)
   282→const agentSkills = await resolveAgentSkills(surreal, identityRecord, workspaceRecord, taskDescription);
   283→// Returns: Array<{ name, content, requiredTools }>
   284→
   285→// Add to context response
   286→context.skills = agentSkills.map(s => ({
   287→  name: s.name,
   288→  instructions: s.content,
   289→}));
   290→
   291→// Filter tools/list to include skill-derived tools
   292→const effectiveTools = union(directGrants, skillDerivedTools);
   293→```
   294→
   295→**Advantages**:
   296→- Brain controls activation (BM25 triggers) and governance (`governs_skill` policy check)
   297→- Implicit tool grants work naturally — `skill_requires` edges filter the MCP `tools/list`
   298→- Fits existing architecture without new adapter surface area
   299→- Skills can reference Brain-specific context (observations, decisions) that file-based skills can't
   300→
   301→**Disadvantages**:
   302→- Skills are injected as prompt context, not as native Agent Skills — the sandbox agent doesn't "know" about them as skills
   303→- No progressive disclosure — all matched skill content lands in the context at once
   304→- Tightly coupled to Brain's MCP protocol
   305→
   306→### Strategy C: Hybrid — Native Skills + MCP Tool Gating (Recommended)
   307→
   308→**Concept**: Use both paths. Materialize skills as `SKILL.md` files for native agent discovery (Strategy A), AND use Brain's MCP layer to enforce tool gating and governance (Strategy B's tool filtering).
   309→
   310→**Flow**:
   311→1. **Pre-session**: Materialize active skills as `SKILL.md` → `setSkillsConfig` (Strategy A)
   312→2. **Pre-session**: Register Brain MCP server → `setMcpConfig` (existing)
   313→3. **Runtime**: Sandbox agent activates skills natively via its own heuristics
   314→4. **Runtime**: When agent calls MCP `tools/list`, Brain returns only tools the agent's skills authorize (`can_use ∪ skill_requires`)
   315→5. **Runtime**: When agent calls a tool, Brain's intent authorization checks the `governs_skill` policy
   316→6. **Post-session**: Skill usage telemetry flows back via `skill_evidence` edges
   317→
   318→**Why this is the best of both worlds**:
   319→
   320→| Concern | Strategy A alone | Strategy B alone | Hybrid (C) |
   321→|---------|-----------------|-----------------|------------|
   322→| Native skill discovery | Yes | No | Yes |
   323→| Brain-controlled availability | Yes (via materialization) | Yes (via MCP context) | Yes (materialization + MCP tool gating) |
   324→| Governance at tool-call time | No | Yes | Yes |
   325→| Progressive disclosure | Yes (agent-native) | No | Yes |
   326→| Cross-agent compatibility | Yes | Brain-only | Yes |
   327→| Implicit tool grants | File-only (`allowed-tools`) | Graph-native (`skill_requires`) | Both |
   328→
   329→---
   330→
   331→## 4. Adapter Changes Required
   332→
   333→### New adapter port: `setSkillsConfig`
   334→
   335→```typescript
   336→// sandbox-adapter.ts — extend SandboxAgentAdapter
   337→export type SkillSource = {
   338→  readonly type: "local" | "github" | "git";
   339→  readonly source: string;
   340→  readonly ref?: string;
   341→  readonly skills?: string[];
   342→  readonly subpath?: string;
   343→};
   344→
   345→export type SkillsConfig = {
   346→  readonly sources: readonly SkillSource[];
   347→};
   348→
   349→export type SandboxAgentAdapter = {
   350→  // ... existing methods ...
   351→  setMcpConfig: (directory: string, name: string, config: McpServerConfig) => Promise<void>;
   352→
   353→  /** Configure skill sources for a directory. Sessions inheriting this cwd discover these skills. */
   354→  setSkillsConfig: (directory: string, name: string, config: SkillsConfig) => Promise<void>;
   355→  deleteSkillsConfig: (directory: string, name: string) => Promise<void>;
   356→};
   357→```
   358→
   359→### Production adapter wiring
   360→
   361→```typescript
   362→// In createSandboxAgentAdapter()
   363→setSkillsConfig: async (directory, name, config) => {
   364→  await sdk.setSkillsConfig({ directory, skillName: name }, config);
   365→},
   366→deleteSkillsConfig: async (directory, name) => {
   367→  await sdk.deleteSkillsConfig({ directory, skillName: name });
   368→},
   369→```
   370→
   371→### Mock adapter extension
   372→
   373→```typescript
   374→// In createMockAdapter()
   375→const skillsConfigs = new Map<string, SkillsConfig>();
   376→
   377→setSkillsConfig: async (directory, name, config) => {
   378→  skillsConfigs.set(`${directory}:${name}`, config);
   379→},
   380→deleteSkillsConfig: async (directory, name) => {
   381→  skillsConfigs.delete(`${directory}:${name}`);
   382→},
   383→```
   384→
   385→---
   386→
   387→## 5. Session Lifecycle Changes
   388→
   389→### Session setup — pass source references to sandbox agent
   390→
   391→Brain passes source references directly to the sandbox agent SDK. The sandbox agent handles downloading and resolving files — Brain never touches skill file content at session time.
   392→
   393→```typescript
   394→// session-lifecycle.ts — in assignTask(), after worktree creation
   395→
   396→// 1. Resolve agent's active skills
   397→const activeSkills = await resolveActiveSkills(input.surreal, identityRecord, workspaceRecord);
   398→
   399→// 2. Register skill sources with Sandbox Agent (it handles file resolution)
   400→if (activeSkills.length > 0) {
   401→  await adapter.setSkillsConfig(worktreePath, "brain-skills", {
   402→    sources: activeSkills.map(skill => skill.source),
   403→  });
   404→}
   405→
   406→// 3. Register Brain MCP server (existing)
   407→await adapter.setMcpConfig(worktreePath, "brain", { ... });
   408→
   409→// 4. Create session (existing)
   410→handle = await adapter.createSession({ ... });
   411→```
   412→
   413→This is significantly simpler than the previous bucket-based approach — no file reads, no filesystem writes, no materialization code. Brain is a metadata + governance layer; the sandbox agent owns file resolution.
   414→
   415→---
   416→
   417→## 6. MCP Tool Gating (Governance Layer)
   418→
   419→Even with native skill activation, Brain's MCP layer enforces which tools the agent can actually call. This is the governance gate that Strategy A alone lacks.
   420→
   421→### Effective tool resolution
   422→
   423→When the sandbox agent calls `tools/list` on Brain's MCP endpoint:
   424→
   425→```typescript
   426→// agent-mcp-route.ts or scope-engine.ts
   427→
   428→// 1. Direct tool grants (existing)
   429→const directGrants = await query("SELECT out AS tool FROM can_use WHERE in = $identity", { identity });
   430→
   431→// 2. Skill-derived tool grants (new)
   432→const skillDerivedTools = await query(`
   433→  SELECT ->skill_requires->mcp_tool AS tool
   434→  FROM possesses
   435→  WHERE in = $identity AND out.status = "active"
   436→`, { identity });
   437→
   438→// 3. Union = effective toolset
   439→const effectiveTools = union(directGrants, skillDerivedTools);
   440→
   441→// 4. Return only these tools in tools/list response
   442→return allWorkspaceTools.filter(t => effectiveTools.has(t.id));
   443→```
   444→
   445→### Policy enforcement at call time
   446→
   447→When the agent calls a tool that came from a skill:
   448→
   449→```typescript
   450→// In tool call handler
   451→const sourceSkill = await query(`
   452→  SELECT <-skill_requires<-skill AS skill
   453→  FROM $tool
   454→  WHERE skill.status = "active"
   455→  AND skill IN (SELECT out FROM possesses WHERE in = $identity)
   456→`, { tool: toolRecord, identity });
   457→
   458→if (sourceSkill) {
   459→  // Check governs_skill policy
   460→  const policy = await query("SELECT in AS policy FROM governs_skill WHERE out = $skill", { skill: sourceSkill.id });
   461→  if (policy) {
   462→    await evaluatePolicy(policy, intent, context);
   463→  }
   464→}
   465→```
   466→
   467→---
   468→
   469→## 7. Import Path: Community Skills → Brain Graph
   470→
   471→Issue #177 specifies compatibility with the 80k+ skills.sh ecosystem. With this integration:
   472→
   473→### Inbound: skills.sh → Brain
   474→
   475→The import flow fetches the SKILL.md (read-only) to extract metadata, then stores the source reference:
   476→
   477→```typescript
   478→// Import flow
   479→// 1. Admin imports a skill from skills.sh
   480→const skillSource = { type: "github", source: "anthropics/skills", skills: ["code-review"], ref: "v1.0" };
   481→
   482→// 2. Brain fetches SKILL.md from source (read-only — not stored)
   483→const rawSkillMd = await fetchRawFile(skillSource, "SKILL.md");
   484→const parsed = parseSkillMd(rawSkillMd);
   485→
   486→// 3. Create graph node with extracted metadata + source reference
   487→await query(`CREATE skill CONTENT $content`, {
   488→  content: {
   489→    name: parsed.name,
   490→    description: parsed.description,
   491→    version: parsed.metadata?.version ?? "1.0",
   492→    status: "draft",  // Human reviews before activation
   493→    workspace: workspaceRecord,
   494→    source: skillSource,  // Source reference — sandbox agent resolves at session time
   495→  }
   496→});
   497→
   498→// 4. LLM analyzes tool requirements → creates skill_requires edges
   499→await analyzeToolRequirements(skill, workspaceTools);
   500→```
   501→
   502→### Outbound: Brain Skill → Sandbox Agent session
   503→
   504→The session setup flow in Section 5 passes source references directly to `setSkillsConfig`. The sandbox agent downloads and resolves files.
   505→
   506→### Flow: skills.sh → Brain → sandbox agent
   507→
   508→```
   509→skills.sh / GitHub registry
   510→  ↓ admin imports: Brain fetches SKILL.md (read-only) to extract metadata
   511→Brain SurrealDB (skill table: name, description, version, source ref)
   512→  ↓ session setup: pass source ref to setSkillsConfig
   513→Sandbox Agent SDK (handles file download/resolution)
   514→  ↓ native discovery (Agent Skills spec)
   515→Agent activates skill based on task context
   516→  ↓ tool calls flow through Brain MCP
   517→Brain enforces governance (intent auth + policy)
   518→```
   519→
   520→**Key simplification**: Brain never stores skill file content. It fetches once on import to extract metadata, then the sandbox agent fetches from source at session time. Version pinning via `ref` ensures reproducibility.
   521→
   522→---
   523→
   524→## 8. What About Custom Tools (Non-MCP)?
   525→
   526→Sandbox Agent's custom tools page describes a simpler pattern: a script + `SKILL.md` that agents invoke directly (not via MCP). Example:
   527→
   528→```
   529→/opt/skills/random-number/
   530→├── random-number.cjs    # Script
   531→└── SKILL.md             # Instructions + invocation command
   532→```
   533→
   534→The `SKILL.md` body contains: `Run: node /opt/skills/random-number/random-number.cjs <min> <max>`
   535→
   536→**Relevance to Brain**: With the source-reference approach, this works naturally. The source repo contains both the SKILL.md and companion scripts. The sandbox agent resolves the full directory from the source reference — Brain doesn't need to know about individual companion files.
   537→
   538→```typescript
   539→// Source reference points to skill directory containing SKILL.md + scripts
   540→{
   541→  type: "github",
   542→  source: "acme-corp/agent-skills",
   543→  ref: "v1.0",
   544→  subpath: "skills/random-number",  // Contains SKILL.md + random-number.cjs
   545→}
   546→```
   547→
   548→The sandbox agent's native resolution handles the full directory structure. No Brain-side materialization needed.
   549→
   550→---
   551→
   552→## 9. Implementation Phases (Aligned with #177)
   553→
   554→| #177 Phase | Integration Work |
   555→|------------|-----------------|
   556→| 1. Schema | No change — schema already supports what's needed |
   557→| 2. CRUD routes | No change |
   558→| 3. Materialization | SKILL.md generation + `setSkillsConfig` registration (Strategy A) |
   559→| 4. Tool requirement analysis | `skill_requires` edges feed `allowed-tools` generation |
   560→| 5. Missing tool resolution | Surface gaps when materializing SKILL.md |
   561→| 6. Context injection | **New**: materialize → `setSkillsConfig` (Strategy A) + MCP tool filtering (Strategy B) |
   562→| 7. Governance | `governs_skill` evaluated at MCP tool-call time |
   563→| 8. Import (skills.sh) | Parse SKILL.md → graph node → round-trip back to SKILL.md for sessions |
   564→| 9. Evolution | Observer proposes updates → new version → rematerialized on next session |
   565→| 10. UI | Show which skills were activated in session telemetry |
   566→
   567→**Phase 6 is the key integration point** — this is where Brain Skills meet Sandbox Agent.
   568→
   569→---
   570→
   571→## 10. Key Decisions (Resolved)
   572→
   573→### D1: Single vs dual activation path — RESOLVED: Hybrid (C)
   574→
   575→Brain controls skill **availability** (which skills are materialized via `setSkillsConfig`), the sandbox agent's LLM controls **activation** (which skills to load based on task context), and Brain enforces **governance** at MCP tool-call time. No BM25 trigger matching needed — the `possesses` relation is the availability gate, the `description` field is the activation signal.
   576→
   577→### D2: Skill storage — RESOLVED: Source references only (no file storage)
   578→
   579→Three options were evaluated:
   580→- `content: string` field: simple but creates lossy round-trip on import
   581→- SurrealDB bucket files: byte-for-byte preservation but adds complexity + experimental `--allow-experimental files` flag
   582→- Source references: store `{ type, source, ref, subpath }` — sandbox agent resolves files at session time
   583→- **Decision**: Source references. Brain stores metadata + source pointer. The sandbox agent already knows how to fetch from GitHub/git/local. Brain's value is governance and metadata, not file hosting.
   584→- **Rationale**: Simplest path to MVP. No file lifecycle management, no bucket setup, no materialization code. Version pinning via `ref` ensures reproducibility.
   585→- **Deferred**: Brain-authored skills (inline content via UI/API) require solving where generated SKILL.md files live. Tracked in #200.
   586→
   587→### D3: `allowed-tools` generation
   588→
   589→The Agent Skills spec's `allowed-tools` field is experimental. Options:
   590→- Generate from `skill_requires` edges (e.g., `Bash(node:*) Read Write`)
   591→- Omit and rely on Brain's MCP `tools/list` filtering instead
   592→- **Recommendation**: Generate `allowed-tools` as a hint for the agent runtime, but enforce via MCP `tools/list` filtering regardless. Belt and suspenders.
   593→
   594→### D4: Script/companion file support — RESOLVED: Handled by sandbox agent
   595→
   596→- The source reference points to a directory containing SKILL.md + any companion files. The sandbox agent's native resolution handles the full directory structure.
   597→- Brain doesn't need to know about individual companion files — it only extracts metadata from the SKILL.md.
   598→
   599→---
   600→
   601→## Sources
   602→
   603→| Source | Type | Accessed |
   604→|--------|------|----------|
   605→| [GitHub Issue #177](https://github.com/conductor-is/brain/issues/177) | Primary | 2026-03-28 |
   606→| [Sandbox Agent Skills Config](https://sandboxagent.dev/docs/skills-config) | Official docs | 2026-03-28 |
   607→| [Sandbox Agent Custom Tools](https://sandboxagent.dev/docs/custom-tools) | Official docs | 2026-03-28 |
   608→| [Sandbox Agent OpenAPI spec](https://sandboxagent.dev/docs/openapi.json) | Official spec | 2026-03-28 |
   609→| [Agent Skills Specification](https://agentskills.io/specification) | Official spec | 2026-03-28 |
   610→| [SurrealDB Files Datamodel](https://surrealdb.com/docs/surrealql/datamodel/files) | Official docs (v3.0) | 2026-03-28 |
   611→| `sandbox-agent@^0.4.0` SDK types (`dist/index.d.ts`) | Installed package | 2026-03-28 |
   612→| `app/src/server/orchestrator/sandbox-adapter.ts` | Codebase | 2026-03-28 |
   613→| `app/src/server/orchestrator/session-lifecycle.ts` | Codebase | 2026-03-28 |
   614→

input.content:
# Journey: Create Agent with Skills

## Persona: Marcus (Workspace Admin)

Technical founder running Osabio. Creates and configures agents for specialized domain tasks (security auditing, code review, compliance checks). Wants agents to have expertise without per-session re-explanation.

## Emotional Arc

```
Start: Intentional        Middle: In Control        End: Confident
"I know what I need"      "This makes sense"        "Agent is ready"
```

## Journey Flow

```
[Trigger: Need a new       [Step 1: Config]         [Step 2: Skills]        [Step 3: Tools]         [Done: Agent Created]
 specialized agent]     ->  Runtime + Identity   ->  Assign Expertise    ->  Review Toolset      ->  Atomic Creation
                            Feels: Focused            Feels: Empowered        Feels: In Control       Feels: Confident
                            "Setting up basics"       "Equipping my agent"    "Everything checks out" "Ready to go"
```

## Step 1: Agent Config

```
+-- Create Agent (Step 1 of 3) ------------------------------------+
|                                                                   |
|  Runtime                                                          |
|  ( ) Sandbox    ( ) External                                      |
|                                                                   |
|  Name                                                             |
|  [security-auditor_________________________]                      |
|                                                                   |
|  Description                                                      |
|  [Performs comprehensive security audits of ____________________] |
|  [code changes before merge. Checks for OWASP___________________]|
|                                                                   |
|  Model                                                            |
|  [claude-sonnet-4-20250514_________________ v]                    |
|                                                                   |
|  Authority Scopes                                                 |
|  +-----------------------------------------------------------+   |
|  | Action         | None | Request | Autonomous |             |   |
|  |----------------|------|---------|------------|             |   |
|  | Create task    |      |   (o)   |            |             |   |
|  | Create decision|      |   (o)   |            |             |   |
|  | Create obs.    |      |         |    (o)     |             |   |
|  +-----------------------------------------------------------+   |
|                                                                   |
|  Sandbox Config (shown when Sandbox selected)                     |
|  Coding Agents: [claude-code_________________ v]                  |
|  Environment Variables: [KEY=VALUE...]                             |
|                                                                   |
|                              [Cancel]  [Next ->]                  |
+-------------------------------------------------------------------+
```

**Emotional state**: Focused, familiar. This is an enhanced version of what they already do.
**Shared artifacts**: `${agent_name}`, `${runtime}`, `${authority_scopes}`

## Step 2: Skills Setup

```
+-- Create Agent (Step 2 of 3) ------------------------------------+
|                                                                   |
|  Assign Skills to "${agent_name}"                                 |
|                                                                   |
|  Select domain expertise for this agent.                          |
|  Skills provide instructions and automatically grant              |
|  required tools.                                                  |
|                                                                   |
|  +-----------------------------------------------------------+   |
|  | [x] security-audit  v1.2                          github   |   |
|  |     Performs comprehensive security audits of code         |   |
|  |     changes. Checks OWASP Top 10, dependency vulns.       |   |
|  +-----------------------------------------------------------+   |
|  | [x] code-review  v2.0                             github   |   |
|  |     Reviews code for quality, maintainability, and         |   |
|  |     adherence to team conventions.                         |   |
|  +-----------------------------------------------------------+   |
|  | [ ] database-migration  v1.0                      git      |   |
|  |     Guides safe database schema migrations with            |   |
|  |     rollback plans and data validation.                    |   |
|  +-----------------------------------------------------------+   |
|  | [ ] api-design  v1.1                              github   |   |
|  |     Designs REST and GraphQL APIs following OpenAPI        |   |
|  |     standards and versioning best practices.               |   |
|  +-----------------------------------------------------------+   |
|                                                                   |
|  2 skills selected                                                |
|                                                                   |
|                    [<- Back]  [Skip]  [Next ->]                   |
+-------------------------------------------------------------------+
```

**Emotional state**: Empowered. "I'm giving my agent real expertise."
**Shared artifacts**: `${selected_skills}`, `${skill_count}`
**Key interaction**: Checking a skill updates the count badge. Unchecking removes it.

## Step 3: Tools Review

```
+-- Create Agent (Step 3 of 3) ------------------------------------+
|                                                                   |
|  Tools for "${agent_name}"                                        |
|                                                                   |
|  SKILL-DERIVED TOOLS (automatic)                                  |
|  These tools are granted by the skills you assigned.              |
|  +-----------------------------------------------------------+   |
|  |  read_file           via security-audit                    |   |
|  |  search_codebase     via security-audit, code-review       |   |
|  |  run_linter          via code-review                       |   |
|  |  check_dependencies  via security-audit                    |   |
|  +-----------------------------------------------------------+   |
|                                                                   |
|  ADDITIONAL TOOLS (manual)                                        |
|  Select extra tools beyond what skills provide.                   |
|  +-----------------------------------------------------------+   |
|  | [x] create_branch                                          |   |
|  | [ ] merge_pr                                               |   |
|  | [x] post_comment                                           |   |
|  | [ ] deploy_staging                                         |   |
|  +-----------------------------------------------------------+   |
|                                                                   |
|  Total effective tools: 6                                         |
|  (4 from skills + 2 additional)                                   |
|                                                                   |
|                    [<- Back]  [Skip]  [Create Agent]              |
+-------------------------------------------------------------------+
```

**Emotional state**: In control. "I can see exactly what this agent can do."
**Shared artifacts**: `${skill_derived_tools}`, `${additional_tools}`, `${total_tools}`
**Key interaction**: Skill-derived section is read-only with "via skill X" labels. Additional section is an editable checklist.

## Step 4: Success Confirmation

```
+-- Agent Created --------------------------------------------------+
|                                                                   |
|  Agent "security-auditor" created successfully.                   |
|                                                                   |
|  Runtime:  Sandbox                                                |
|  Skills:   2 (security-audit, code-review)                        |
|  Tools:    6 (4 skill-derived + 2 additional)                     |
|                                                                   |
|                              [View Agent]  [Create Another]       |
+-------------------------------------------------------------------+
```

**Emotional state**: Confident, satisfied. "My agent is equipped and ready."

## Error Paths

### No skills exist in workspace
Step 2 shows an empty state: "No skills in this workspace yet. Create skills in the Skill Library to assign them to agents." with a link to skill library. Skip button is prominent.

### Agent name already taken
Step 1 shows inline validation on the name field when blurred: "An agent named 'security-auditor' already exists."

### Skill becomes deprecated between step 2 and step 3
Edge case. At creation time (step 3 submit), the transaction validates all selected skills are still active. If any are deprecated, show error: "Skill 'security-audit' was deprecated since you started. Please go back and update your selection."

### External agent path
Steps 2 and 3 are shown with a muted banner: "Skills and skill-derived tools are only used by sandbox agents. You can still select additional tools." Skip button is the primary action.

input.content:
journey:
  name: "Create Agent with Skills"
  goal: "Workspace admin creates a new agent equipped with domain expertise and the correct toolset"
  persona: "Marcus (Workspace Admin) — technical founder, creates specialized agents"

  emotional_arc:
    start: "Intentional — knows what kind of agent is needed"
    middle: "Empowered — actively equipping the agent with expertise"
    end: "Confident — agent is fully configured and ready"

steps:
  - id: 1
    name: "Agent Config"
    action: "Fill in runtime, name, description, model, authority scopes, sandbox config"

    shared_artifacts:
      - name: "agent_name"
        source: "Step 1 name input field"
        displayed_as: "${agent_name}"
        consumers: ["Step 2 heading", "Step 3 heading", "Success confirmation"]
      - name: "runtime"
        source: "Step 1 runtime radio selection"
        displayed_as: "${runtime}"
        consumers: ["Step 2 external-agent banner", "Step 3 skill-derived section visibility", "Success confirmation"]
      - name: "authority_scopes"
        source: "Step 1 authority scope matrix"
        displayed_as: "${authority_scopes}"
        consumers: ["Agent creation transaction"]

    emotional_state:
      entry: "Focused — familiar form, enhanced version of existing wizard"
      exit: "Grounded — identity established, ready to add expertise"

    integration_checkpoint: |
      Agent name must be unique within workspace (validated on blur).
      Runtime selection determines conditional visibility in steps 2 and 3.

    gherkin: |
      Scenario: Configure sandbox agent identity
        Given Marcus is on the Create Agent page
        When he selects "Sandbox" runtime and enters name "security-auditor"
        And he fills in description, model, and authority scopes
        Then the Next button becomes active
        And sandbox-specific config fields are visible

  - id: 2
    name: "Skills Setup"
    action: "Browse and select skills from workspace skill catalog"

    shared_artifacts:
      - name: "selected_skills"
        source: "Step 2 skill checklist selections"
        displayed_as: "${selected_skills}"
        consumers: ["Step 3 skill-derived tools section", "Agent creation transaction", "Success confirmation"]
      - name: "skill_count"
        source: "Derived from selected_skills.length"
        displayed_as: "${skill_count}"
        consumers: ["Step 2 count badge", "Success confirmation"]
      - name: "workspace_skills"
        source: "GET /api/workspaces/:id/skills?status=active"
        displayed_as: "Skill checklist items"
        consumers: ["Step 2 checklist"]

    emotional_state:
      entry: "Curious — exploring what expertise is available"
      exit: "Empowered — agent now has domain knowledge"

    integration_checkpoint: |
      All displayed skills must have status=active.
      Selected skills array is preserved when navigating back from step 3.
      Empty state shown when workspace has no active skills.

    gherkin: |
      Scenario: Assign skills to agent
        Given Marcus is on Step 2 of Create Agent for "security-auditor"
        And the workspace has 4 active skills
        When he checks "security-audit" and "code-review"
        Then the count badge shows "2 skills selected"
        And the Next button is active

      Scenario: Skip skills assignment
        Given Marcus is on Step 2 of Create Agent
        When he clicks Skip
        Then he advances to Step 3 with zero skills selected

      Scenario: No skills available
        Given Marcus is on Step 2 of Create Agent
        And the workspace has no active skills
        Then an empty state message is shown with a link to the Skill Library
        And Skip is the primary action

  - id: 3
    name: "Tools Review"
    action: "Review skill-derived tools and optionally select additional tools"

    shared_artifacts:
      - name: "skill_derived_tools"
        source: "Resolved from selected_skills -> skill_requires -> mcp_tool"
        displayed_as: "${skill_derived_tools}"
        consumers: ["Step 3 skill-derived tools section (read-only)", "Success confirmation"]
      - name: "additional_tools"
        source: "Step 3 additional tools checklist selections"
        displayed_as: "${additional_tools}"
        consumers: ["Agent creation transaction (can_use edges)", "Success confirmation"]
      - name: "total_tools"
        source: "Derived from union(skill_derived_tools, additional_tools)"
        displayed_as: "${total_tools}"
        consumers: ["Step 3 summary line", "Success confirmation"]

    emotional_state:
      entry: "Observant — reviewing what skills imply for tool access"
      exit: "In control — full visibility of agent capabilities"

    integration_checkpoint: |
      Skill-derived tools section correctly reflects skill_requires edges for selected skills.
      Each skill-derived tool shows "via skill X" label.
      Tools appearing in both skill-derived and additional are not double-counted.
      Additional tools section shows workspace mcp_tool catalog minus skill-derived tools.

    gherkin: |
      Scenario: Review skill-derived tools
        Given Marcus selected "security-audit" and "code-review" in Step 2
        When he arrives at Step 3
        Then the skill-derived section shows 4 tools with source skill labels
        And the skill-derived tools are read-only (not editable)

      Scenario: Select additional tools
        Given Marcus is on Step 3 with 4 skill-derived tools
        When he checks "create_branch" and "post_comment" in Additional Tools
        Then the total effective tools summary shows "6 (4 from skills + 2 additional)"

  - id: 4
    name: "Create Agent"
    action: "Submit agent creation with all configuration"

    shared_artifacts:
      - name: "agent_record"
        source: "POST /api/agents (atomic transaction)"
        displayed_as: "Success confirmation"
        consumers: ["Agent list page", "Agent detail page"]

    emotional_state:
      entry: "Anticipating — about to commit"
      exit: "Confident — agent exists and is fully equipped"

    integration_checkpoint: |
      Atomic transaction creates: agent record, identity, member_of edge,
      authorized_to edges, possesses edges, can_use edges, proxy token (if external).
      All selected skills must still be active at transaction time.
      Success confirmation shows full summary.

    gherkin: |
      Scenario: Successful agent creation with skills
        Given Marcus has completed steps 1-3 with name "security-auditor", 2 skills, and 2 additional tools
        When he clicks "Create Agent"
        Then the agent is created atomically with all relations
        And success confirmation shows runtime, skills, and tools summary

      Scenario: Skill deprecated during wizard flow
        Given Marcus selected "security-audit" in Step 2
        And the skill was deprecated after he moved to Step 3
        When he clicks "Create Agent"
        Then an error explains the skill was deprecated
        And he can navigate back to Step 2 to update selections

integration_validation:
  shared_artifact_consistency:
    - artifact: "agent_name"
      must_match_across: [1, 2, 3, 4]
      failure_message: "Agent name displayed in step headings must match the Step 1 input"
    - artifact: "selected_skills"
      must_match_across: [2, 3, 4]
      failure_message: "Skills selected in Step 2 must drive the tool-derived section in Step 3 and the creation transaction"
    - artifact: "runtime"
      must_match_across: [1, 2, 3]
      failure_message: "Runtime selection in Step 1 must control conditional content in Steps 2 and 3"
